q: What is the JVM and how does it differ from the JRE and JDK?
a: The JVM (Java Virtual Machine) is the runtime engine that executes Java bytecode on any platform, providing memory management, garbage collection, and JIT compilation. The JRE (Java Runtime Environment) bundles the JVM plus the standard class libraries needed to run programs. The JDK (Java Development Kit) is a superset of the JRE that adds `javac`, `jdb`, and other development tools â€” you develop with the JDK and deploy with the JRE.
fj: JVM and JRE are basically the same thing; JDK is only needed for advanced features.
fs: JVM executes bytecode; JRE = JVM + runtime libraries; JDK = JRE + compiler + dev tools.

q: How does the JIT compiler improve Java performance at runtime?
a: The JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler identifies 'hot' methods and compiles them from bytecode into native machine code on the fly, eliminating interpretation overhead for repeated calls. HotSpot uses a tiered model: code first runs interpreted, then C1 produces lightly-optimised native code, then C2 applies aggressive optimisations â€” inlining, escape analysis, loop unrolling â€” on the hottest paths. Long-running Java processes warm up slowly but eventually approach native-compiled performance.
fj: JIT compiles the whole program to native code when the JVM starts.
fs: JIT compiles hot paths to native code at runtime: interpretation â†’ C1 â†’ C2, each tier more aggressive. That's why throughput climbs after warmup.

q: Explain the main Java GC algorithms and when to choose each.
a: Java ships several collectors: Serial GC (single-threaded, tiny heaps), Parallel GC (throughput-first, multi-threaded stop-the-world), G1 GC (default since Java 9 â€” region-based, configurable pause goals), and ZGC / Shenandoah (sub-millisecond concurrent compaction, production-ready from Java 15+). G1 is the go-to for most server workloads; ZGC or Shenandoah suit latency-sensitive services with multi-GB heaps that must keep p99 pause < 5 ms.
fj: There is just one GC â€” you cannot change it.
fs: G1 for balanced throughput/latency; ZGC or Shenandoah for sub-ms pause SLAs on large heaps. Profile GC logs first before changing collectors.

q: What is the difference between primitives and wrapper types, and what is autoboxing?
a: Primitive types (`int`, `long`, `double`, etc.) are stored by value on the stack with no object overhead. Wrapper types (`Integer`, `Long`, `Double`) are heap objects that add nullability, generic support, and utility methods. Autoboxing is the compiler's automatic conversion: `Integer x = 5` boxes the `int`; `int y = x` unboxes it. Autoboxing inside tight loops silently allocates heap objects and can cause significant GC pressure.
fj: Wrappers and primitives are interchangeable â€” Java handles everything for you.
fs: Primitives are stack-allocated values; wrappers are heap objects. Autoboxing in hot loops creates silent allocation pressure â€” prefer primitives there.

q: What is the Integer caching trap with `==`?
a: The JVM caches `Integer` instances for values -128 to 127, so `Integer.valueOf(100) == Integer.valueOf(100)` returns `true` because both references point to the same cached object. Outside that range new heap objects are allocated, so `Integer.valueOf(200) == Integer.valueOf(200)` returns `false`. This makes `==` unreliable for `Integer` comparison â€” always use `.equals()` or unbox to `int`.
fj: Integer comparison with `==` always works fine.
fs: Integer cache covers -128 to 127. Outside that range `==` compares references, not values. Always use `.equals()` for `Integer` equality.

followups.q: Can the Integer cache upper bound be expanded?
followups.a: Yes â€” raise it via `-XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=<n>`. The lower bound of -128 is fixed by the JLS.

q: Why is `String` immutable and what is the string pool?
a: `String` objects are immutable â€” once created their character data never changes â€” allowing the JVM to safely share instances via the string pool (interned strings in the heap). Writing `String s = "hello"` causes the JVM to check the pool first and reuse an existing instance if present. Immutability also makes `String` inherently thread-safe and allows `hashCode()` to be lazily cached. For mutable string construction use `StringBuilder` (single-thread) or `StringBuffer` (thread-safe).
fj: String immutability is just a historical quirk; it doesn't matter for modern Java.
fs: Immutability enables string pool sharing, thread safety, and `hashCode` caching. Use `StringBuilder` when building strings in a loop.

q: What is the contract between `equals()` and `hashCode()`?
a: If `a.equals(b)` is `true`, then `a.hashCode()` MUST equal `b.hashCode()`. The converse is not required â€” collisions are allowed. Breaking this contract â€” overriding `equals()` without `hashCode()` â€” causes `HashMap` and `HashSet` to silently malfunction: equal objects map to different buckets and are never found. IDEs and Lombok can generate both methods together to ensure consistency.
fj: I only override `equals()` â€” `hashCode()` doesn't matter unless I explicitly use a Map.
fs: Override both together. Equal objects MUST have equal hashCodes; every hash-based collection depends on this.

q: What is the difference between `==` and `equals()` in Java?
a: `==` compares references for objects (or values for primitives) â€” it answers 'are these the same object in memory?'. `equals()` compares logical content: `String.equals()` and well-implemented domain objects test whether two instances represent the same value. The default `Object.equals()` falls back to `==`, so classes that do not override it use reference equality. Always use `equals()` (or `Objects.equals(a, b)` to handle nulls) for semantic equality.
fj: `==` compares content for objects; it only differs for primitives.
fs: `==` is reference identity for objects. Override `equals()` for value semantics; use `Objects.equals(a, b)` to guard against nulls.

q: Compare ArrayList, LinkedList, HashMap, and TreeMap â€” Big-O and when to use each.
a: `ArrayList`: O(1) amortised get/set by index, O(n) insert/delete in middle; best for random access. `LinkedList`: O(n) get by index, O(1) insert/delete at a known node; useful only as a deque. `HashMap`: O(1) average get/put/remove; use when fast key lookup with no ordering is needed. `TreeMap`: O(log n) for all operations; maintains keys in sorted order â€” use when sorted iteration or range queries are required.
fj: LinkedList is faster than ArrayList because it never copies elements.
fs: `ArrayList` beats `LinkedList` in nearly every benchmark due to CPU cache locality. Use `TreeMap` only when sorted-key iteration is actually required.

q: How does `HashMap` work internally â€” buckets, collisions, treeification?
a: `HashMap` stores entries in an array of buckets indexed by `(n-1) & hash(key)`. Collisions are stored as a linked list in the bucket. Since Java 8, when a bucket's chain exceeds `TREEIFY_THRESHOLD` (8) AND total capacity is at least `MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY` (64), the chain becomes a red-black tree giving O(log n) worst-case lookup. When entries drop below `UNTREEIFY_THRESHOLD` (6) the tree reverts to a list. The table doubles when load factor (default 0.75) is exceeded.
fj: HashMap stores everything in a flat array â€” the hash code IS the index.
fs: Bucket = `(n-1) & hash`. Chains treeify at 8 entries (O(log n)); untreeify at 6. Load factor 0.75 triggers capacity doubling.

followups.q: Why is the default load factor 0.75?
followups.a: It balances space and time: at 0.75 the Poisson-expected entries per bucket is about 0.5, minimising collisions while not wasting memory.

followups.q: What happens if your key's `hashCode()` always returns the same value?
followups.a: Every entry lands in one bucket, degrading all lookups to O(log n) after treeification. This is a hash-flooding attack that can be exploited with adversarial inputs.

q: What is type erasure in Java generics?
a: Generics are a compile-time feature: the compiler checks type constraints then erases them â€” `List<String>` becomes `List` in bytecode and casts are inserted at use sites. This means you cannot use `new T()`, `T.class`, `instanceof List<String>`, or `new T[10]` at runtime because the type parameter is gone. Type erasure preserves backward compatibility with pre-generics code. Pass a `Class<T>` token to a method when you genuinely need the type at runtime.
fj: Java generics work like C++ templates â€” the JVM has full type information at runtime.
fs: Generics exist only at compile time. After erasure the JVM sees raw types â€” `instanceof T` and `new T[]` are impossible at runtime.

q: Explain PECS (Producer Extends Consumer Super) for generics wildcards.
a: PECS guides wildcard choice. Use `? extends T` (upper bound) when you only READ from the collection â€” it is a 'producer' of `T` values. Use `? super T` (lower bound) when you only WRITE into the collection â€” it 'consumes' `T` values. If you both read and write, use a concrete type parameter with no wildcard. `Collections.copy(List<? super T> dest, List<? extends T> src)` is the canonical example: `src` produces, `dest` consumes.
fj: Just use `?` wildcard everywhere for maximum flexibility.
fs: Read-only (produces) â†’ `? extends T`. Write (consumes) â†’ `? super T`. Both â†’ concrete `<T>`. Unbounded `?` gives read-only `Object` semantics.

q: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions?
a: Checked exceptions extend `Exception` (excluding `RuntimeException`) and must be declared in the method signature or caught â€” the compiler enforces this. They model recoverable conditions the caller should handle (e.g., `IOException`, `SQLException`). Unchecked exceptions extend `RuntimeException` and represent programming errors (e.g., `NullPointerException`, `IllegalArgumentException`). Modern practice favours unchecked exceptions to avoid exception-swallowing and noisy `throws` clauses; use checked only when the caller has a realistic recovery path.
fj: Checked exceptions are better because the compiler forces you to handle them.
fs: Checked = compiler-enforced recoverable errors. Unchecked = programmer bugs. Modern APIs prefer unchecked to avoid swallowed exceptions and cluttered signatures.

q: How does `try-with-resources` work and what must a resource implement?
a: `try-with-resources` (Java 7) automatically calls `close()` on declared resources at the end of the block in reverse declaration order, even if an exception is thrown. The resource must implement `java.lang.AutoCloseable`. If both the try body and `close()` throw, the body exception propagates and the close exception is attached as a suppressed exception via `Throwable.addSuppressed()`. This replaces the error-prone `finally` + null-check pattern.
fj: You still need a `finally` block to close resources â€” `try-with-resources` is just optional syntax.
fs: Resources are closed in reverse declaration order in a compiler-generated `finally`; close exceptions are suppressed, not lost. Resource must implement `AutoCloseable`.

q: What are the key differences between an `interface` and an `abstract class`?
a: An `interface` defines a contract; since Java 8 it can have `default` and `static` methods, and since Java 9 `private` methods. A class may implement many interfaces but extend only one class. An `abstract class` can hold instance state (fields), constructors, and concrete methods. Use an interface for multiple-inheritance-of-type and a pure contract; use an abstract class when subclasses share state or a base implementation.
fj: Interfaces are just abstract classes without any code.
fs: Interfaces = contract, multiple implementation, no instance state (Java 8+ `default`/`static` methods ok). Abstract class = shared state + partial implementation, single inheritance.

q: What are `default` methods on interfaces and why were they added?
a: `default` methods (Java 8) let interfaces provide a concrete method body, enabling backward-compatible evolution. The JDK added `forEach`, `stream()`, and `spliterator()` to `Collection` without breaking existing implementations. If a class implements two interfaces with conflicting `default` methods the compiler forces an explicit override. A class method always wins over a `default`; a more-specific interface wins over a less-specific one.
fj: `default` methods make interfaces the same as abstract classes.
fs: `default` methods let library authors evolve interfaces without breaking implementors. Conflicts must be resolved in the implementing class; class method always beats interface default.

q: What is the difference between method overloading and method overriding?
a: Overloading is compile-time polymorphism: multiple methods share a name but differ in parameter types; the compiler resolves the call based on the static type of arguments. Overriding is runtime polymorphism: a subclass redefines a method with the same signature; the JVM dispatches to the most-derived implementation via the vtable. Overriding requires the same name, same parameter types, a covariant return type, and no narrower access modifier.
fj: Overloading and overriding both pick the method at runtime based on the object's type.
fs: Overloading = compile-time dispatch on static type. Overriding = runtime dispatch via vtable on actual object type.

q: What do `final`, `finally`, and `finalize` mean?
a: `final` on a variable means the reference cannot be reassigned (the object may still be mutated); on a method it cannot be overridden; on a class it cannot be subclassed. `finally` is a block that always runs after try-catch (except `System.exit()` or JVM crash). `finalize()` was an `Object` method called by the GC before collection â€” deprecated in Java 9 and removed in Java 18 because it is unpredictable; use `try-with-resources` or `java.lang.ref.Cleaner` instead.
fj: `finalize()` is like a destructor â€” I call it to release resources when done.
fs: `final` = immutable ref / no override / no extend. `finally` = guaranteed cleanup. `finalize()` removed in Java 18 â€” use `try-with-resources` or `Cleaner`.

q: How do you design a truly immutable class in Java?
a: Declare the class `final`. Declare all fields `private final`. Provide no setters. Ensure deep immutability: if a field references a mutable object (e.g., a `List`), copy it defensively in the constructor and return an unmodifiable view or copy from accessors. Initialise all fields in the constructor. With these rules the object's state cannot change after construction, making it inherently thread-safe.
fj: Making all fields `final` is enough to make a class immutable.
fs: True immutability: `final` class + `private final` fields + no setters + DEFENSIVE COPIES of mutable types in the constructor and accessors.

q: What is the difference between `Comparable` and `Comparator`?
a: `Comparable<T>` is implemented by the class itself (`compareTo(T o)`) to define its natural ordering â€” intrinsic to the object. `Comparator<T>` is a separate functional interface defining an external ordering â€” extrinsic. Use `Comparable` when there is one obvious natural ordering (e.g., `String`, `Integer`). Use `Comparator` when you need multiple orderings or cannot modify the class. Java 8 added `Comparator.comparing()` factories and chained `thenComparing()` for fluent multi-key sorting.
fj: `Comparator` is the newer replacement for `Comparable`.
fs: `Comparable` = single natural order baked in. `Comparator` = external, swappable order. Use `Comparator.comparing().thenComparing()` for multi-key sorts.

q: How does the Streams API work? What are intermediate vs terminal operations?
a: A `Stream` is a lazy, declarative pipeline over a data source. Intermediate operations (`filter`, `map`, `flatMap`, `sorted`, `distinct`) return a new stream and trigger no computation. Terminal operations (`collect`, `forEach`, `reduce`, `count`, `findFirst`) pull data through the pipeline. Streams are consumed once; reuse throws `IllegalStateException`. Parallel streams split work across the common `ForkJoinPool` â€” only beneficial for CPU-bound, stateless, independently splittable large datasets.
fj: Streams eagerly process each step in order â€” first all `filter`, then all `map`.
fs: Pipelines are lazy: no work until a terminal op fires. Elements flow through the full chain one-by-one (fusion). Parallel streams are not always faster.

q: What are lambda expressions and functional interfaces in Java?
a: A lambda is an anonymous function â€” a concise way to pass behaviour. Any interface with exactly one abstract method (a SAM) is a functional interface and can be instantiated with a lambda. `java.util.function` provides: `Function<T,R>`, `Predicate<T>`, `Consumer<T>`, `Supplier<T>`, `BiFunction<T,U,R>`. Mark custom SAMs with `@FunctionalInterface` so the compiler enforces the constraint. Lambdas capture effectively-final local variables from the enclosing scope.
fj: Lambdas are just shorter anonymous inner class syntax with the same performance.
fs: Lambdas implement functional interfaces via `invokedynamic` â€” lighter than anonymous inner classes. `@FunctionalInterface` enforces SAM contract at compile time.

q: What is `Optional` and what are its correct vs incorrect uses?
a: `Optional<T>` (Java 8) is a container that may or may not hold a non-null value, designed to model explicit absence in return types. Correct: method return type when absence is meaningful. Incorrect: field type (bloats objects), method parameter (use overloading instead), or collection element. Prefer `orElse`, `orElseGet`, `map`, `ifPresent` over calling `get()` without `isPresent()` â€” that defeats the purpose.
fj: `Optional` is a null replacement â€” use it everywhere to eliminate NPEs.
fs: `Optional` is for return types signalling 'this might not exist'. Never use as a field, parameter, or collection element.

q: Is Java pass-by-value or pass-by-reference?
a: Java is strictly pass-by-value. For primitives the value itself is copied. For objects the value of the reference (a pointer to the heap object) is copied â€” the method gets its own copy of the reference pointing to the same heap object. A method can mutate the object's state via the copied reference, but reassigning the reference inside the method does not affect the caller's variable.
fj: Java is pass-by-reference for objects â€” that's why methods can modify them.
fs: Always pass-by-value. Objects pass a copy of the reference â€” the method shares the object but cannot reroute the caller's variable.

q: What are Java records (Java 16+) and when should you use them?
a: Records are a concise syntax for immutable data carriers. `record Point(int x, int y) {}` auto-generates `private final` fields, a canonical constructor, `equals()`, `hashCode()`, and `toString()` based on all components. Records are implicitly `final` and cannot have extra instance fields. Use them for DTOs, value objects, and method return types grouping multiple values. You can add compact constructors for validation, override auto-generated methods, and add instance methods.
fj: Records are just annotated POJOs â€” same as Lombok `@Data`.
fs: Records = immutable, auto-boilerplate data classes. Implicitly final, components are private final with accessors auto-generated. Perfect for DTOs and value objects.

q: What are sealed classes (Java 17+) and how do they enable exhaustive pattern matching?
a: A sealed class restricts its permitted subclasses via the `permits` clause; each must be `final`, `sealed`, or `non-sealed`. The compiler knows the complete set of subtypes, enabling exhaustive `switch` pattern matching without a `default` branch â€” adding a new permitted subclass makes any non-exhaustive switch a compile error. Combined with records they model algebraic data types, explicitly enumerating every valid state.
fj: Sealed classes are just abstract classes with a `final` modifier â€” nothing new.
fs: Sealed classes give the compiler a closed subtype set for exhaustive `switch`. Adding a new permitted subclass â†’ compile error on non-exhaustive switches.

q: What is `var` (Java 10+) and what are its limitations?
a: `var` enables local variable type inference â€” the compiler infers the type from the initialiser at compile time. It is syntactic sugar: the bytecode is identical to an explicit declaration. Restrictions: only valid for local variables with an initialiser, enhanced-for variables, and traditional for-loop indexes â€” not for fields, method parameters, return types, or catch variables. Cannot initialise with `null` alone (no type to infer). Cannot use with poly expressions (lambdas, method references) as the sole initialiser.
fj: `var` makes Java dynamically typed like Python.
fs: `var` is compile-time inference, not dynamic typing. Forbidden for fields, parameters, return types, and `null`-only initialisers.

q: What does `synchronized` do and what are its performance trade-offs?
a: `synchronized` acquires the intrinsic monitor lock before entering a block or method, ensuring mutual exclusion and memory visibility (all prior writes flush to main memory). It is reentrant. Downsides: coarse-grained locking causes contention, no timeout, no fairness guarantees. Java 5+ `ReentrantLock` offers `tryLock` with timeout, interruptible waits, and optional fairness. Prefer `java.util.concurrent` utilities over raw `synchronized` for complex scenarios.
fj: `synchronized` makes a method fully thread-safe with no drawbacks.
fs: `synchronized` = mutual exclusion + memory visibility, but coarse locking causes contention. Prefer `ReentrantLock` for timeouts/fairness or higher-level `java.util.concurrent` tools.

q: What is `volatile` and when must you use it?
a: Declaring a field `volatile` gives two JMM guarantees: (1) Visibility â€” every write flushes to main memory and every read fetches from main memory, bypassing CPU caches. (2) Ordering â€” reads and writes cannot be reordered past other `volatile` accesses. Use `volatile` for simple stop-running flags or for safe publication of fully constructed objects. It does NOT provide atomicity for compound operations like `i++` â€” use `AtomicInteger` for that.
fj: `volatile` makes a variable thread-safe â€” I can use it instead of `synchronized`.
fs: `volatile` = visibility + ordering, NOT atomicity. `i++` is still a race. Use `AtomicInteger` for compound ops; `volatile` for simple flags or safe publication.

q: What is the Java Memory Model and what is the happens-before relation?
a: The Java Memory Model (JMM, JLS Â§17.4) defines when one thread's writes are guaranteed visible to another. Happens-before is the formal guarantee: if A happens-before B, A's effects are visible to B. Key edges: within one thread, program order; a monitor unlock happens-before any subsequent lock of the same monitor; a `volatile` write happens-before any subsequent read of the same field; `Thread.start()` happens-before any action in the started thread. Without a happens-before chain the JVM and CPU are free to reorder and cache writes invisibly.
fj: If I write a variable in thread A and read it in thread B, thread B always sees the latest value.
fs: Visibility is only guaranteed through a happens-before chain: unlockâ†’lock, volatile writeâ†’read, thread.start()/join(). Without one, stale cached values are valid JVM behaviour.

q: What is the double-checked locking pitfall and how do you fix it?
a: Without `volatile`, the JIT can reorder the write to the instance reference before the object is fully initialised â€” a second thread may see a non-null but incompletely constructed object. The fix is to declare the field `volatile`, which establishes a happens-before between constructor completion and any subsequent read. Alternatively, the Initialization-on-Demand Holder idiom uses a static inner class; JVM class loading is inherently safe by the JLS spec.
fj: Double-checked locking is always safe as long as the `synchronized` block is there.
fs: Without `volatile`, JIT reordering can expose a partially constructed object. Fix: `volatile` field, or use the holder-class idiom (JVM class-loading is safe by spec).

q: What is `ExecutorService` and why use it instead of creating threads directly?
a: `ExecutorService` manages a pool of reusable threads, decoupling task submission from execution. Creating a new `Thread` per task is expensive and risks unbounded thread counts. Key factories: `newFixedThreadPool(n)` â€” bounded concurrency; `newCachedThreadPool()` â€” expands on demand (risky); `newSingleThreadExecutor()` â€” serial ordered execution; `newScheduledThreadPool(n)` â€” delayed/periodic. Always call `shutdown()`. Java 21 virtual threads (`Thread.ofVirtual()`) simplify I/O-bound work without pool-size tuning.
fj: I just create a new `Thread` per request â€” it's simpler.
fs: Thread-per-request = unbounded threads and high creation cost. `ExecutorService` bounds concurrency and reuses threads. Java 21 virtual threads eliminate pool-size tuning for I/O-heavy work.

q: How does `ConcurrentHashMap` differ from `Collections.synchronizedMap`?
a: In Java 8+ `ConcurrentHashMap` uses a node array with CAS for non-contending writes and per-bucket `synchronized` blocks only on collision. Reads are lock-free (volatile node reads). Multiple threads can write to different buckets concurrently. `Collections.synchronizedMap` wraps every method in a single coarse lock â€” all operations serialise on one monitor. `ConcurrentHashMap` also never throws `ConcurrentModificationException` and provides atomic compound operations like `computeIfAbsent`.
fj: `synchronizedMap` and `ConcurrentHashMap` are equally thread-safe â€” just pick one.
fs: `ConcurrentHashMap`: lock-free reads, per-bucket CAS/sync writes, no global lock. `synchronizedMap`: one coarse lock serialises every read and write.

q: What are atomic classes (`AtomicInteger`, `AtomicReference`, `LongAdder`) and when do you use them?
a: The `java.util.concurrent.atomic` package provides variables supporting atomic compound operations via CPU-level CAS instructions, avoiding locks. `AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet()` is atomically safe â€” no race. `AtomicReference<T>` enables lock-free reference swapping. `LongAdder` outperforms `AtomicLong` under high contention by striping the counter across cells; threads increment different cells and `sum()` combines them. Use atomics for counters, flags, and simple state machines; use locks when the critical section spans multiple variables.
fj: Replace every shared `int` with `AtomicInteger` and everything is thread-safe.
fs: Atomics use CAS for lock-free compound ops. `LongAdder` beats `AtomicLong` at high write contention. If critical section touches multiple variables you still need a lock.

q: How can deadlock occur in Java and how do you prevent it?
a: Deadlock occurs when two or more threads each hold a lock the other needs, forming a circular wait. Prevention: (1) Consistent global lock-acquisition order â€” always acquire locks in the same sequence. (2) `tryLock` with timeout (`ReentrantLock`) â€” back off if you cannot acquire both. (3) Avoid nested locking where possible. (4) Prefer `java.util.concurrent` constructs designed to avoid deadlock. Detection: `jstack` thread dumps show `BLOCKED` threads with lock chains.
fj: Deadlock only happens in poorly written code â€” just be careful with `synchronized`.
fs: Deadlock = circular lock dependency. Prevent by enforcing global lock order, using `tryLock` with timeout, or eliminating nested locking.

q: What is `ThreadLocal` and what memory-leak pitfall must you know?
a: `ThreadLocal<T>` provides each thread its own isolated copy of a variable via `get()`/`set()`/`remove()`. Common uses: per-request context, `SimpleDateFormat` instances, Spring's `SecurityContextHolder`. Critical pitfall: thread pool threads are long-lived and reused. If you `set()` a `ThreadLocal` and never call `remove()`, the value lingers on the thread â€” visible to the next request and causing a memory leak because `ThreadLocalMap` holds a strong reference to the value. Always call `remove()` in a `finally` block.
fj: `ThreadLocal` variables are automatically cleaned up when the method returns.
fs: Thread pool threads never die â€” a `ThreadLocal` set without `remove()` leaks context to the next request. Always call `remove()` in `finally`.

q: What is `CompletableFuture` and when do you use `thenApply` vs `thenCompose`?
a: `CompletableFuture<T>` (Java 8) is a composable non-blocking async computation. `thenApply(Function<T,U>)` transforms the result synchronously â€” analogous to `Stream.map()`. `thenCompose(Function<T, CompletableFuture<U>>)` flat-maps: when the next step is itself async it avoids the nested `CompletableFuture<CompletableFuture<U>>` you would get with `thenApply`. `thenCombine` merges two independent futures running in parallel. Use `exceptionally` or `handle` for error recovery. All methods have `*Async` variants for off-thread execution.
fj: `thenApply` and `thenCompose` do the same thing â€” just pick one.
fs: `thenApply` = sync transform (map). `thenCompose` = flat-map when next step is async (avoids nested futures). `thenCombine` = merge two independent futures.

followups.a: The completing thread runs the callback inline. Use `thenApplyAsync()` with an optional custom `Executor` to run the callback on a separate thread.

q: What causes `ConcurrentModificationException` and how do you avoid it?
a: `ConcurrentModificationException` is thrown when you structurally modify a collection while iterating it. The collection tracks a `modCount` and the iterator checks it on every step. Safe alternatives: (1) `Iterator.remove()` to remove during iteration. (2) `list.removeIf(predicate)` (Java 8). (3) Collect removals in a list and call `removeAll` after. (4) `CopyOnWriteArrayList` for read-heavy collections. `ConcurrentHashMap` iterators never throw this â€” they provide a weakly-consistent view.
fj: I can call `list.remove(item)` inside an enhanced for-loop safely.
fs: Structural modification during iteration throws CME. Fix: use `Iterator.remove()`, `removeIf()`, or collect-then-remove.

q: What is the risk of catching `Exception` too broadly?
a: Catching `Exception` masks programming bugs: `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, and `ClassCastException` all extend `RuntimeException` which extends `Exception`. A broad catch swallows these, logs an unhelpful message, and lets the application continue in a corrupt state. Always catch the most specific type you can handle. Top-level framework handlers that must catch broadly should re-throw after logging or wrap in a domain exception. Never use an empty catch block.
fj: Catching `Exception` is safer â€” you handle every possible error at once.
fs: Broad `catch (Exception e)` swallows `RuntimeException`s that signal real bugs. Catch specific types; let unrecoverable errors propagate. Never empty-catch.

q: What is the risk of mutable shared static state in concurrent Java code?
a: A `static` mutable field is shared across all instances and all threads. Without synchronisation concurrent reads and writes cause data races: updates may be invisible to other threads (JMM visibility) or multiple threads interleave non-atomically (e.g., `++` is read-modify-write). Typical failures: counters under-count, caches return stale data, or state machines reach invalid states. Fix by using `AtomicInteger`/`AtomicReference`, `volatile` for simple flags, or `synchronized` â€” or eliminate mutable shared state by designing for immutability.
fj: A `static` field is fine in concurrent code as long as only one thread writes it.
fs: Mutable `static` fields are shared global state â€” every thread races on them. Use `AtomicXxx`, `volatile`, or `synchronized`; prefer stateless, immutable designs.
